required.title: [Read Aloud] Various fables and folktales involving moral dilemmas

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Stories about courage in the face of peer pressure

competence_markers.description: The student can articulate that doing right when it costs something is more significant than doing right when it costs nothing
The student can identify situations where good people disagree and explain why both sides have a point
The student can distinguish between equal treatment and fair treatment with specific examples
The student demonstrates the habit of asking "why did this person make this choice?" before judging it
The student can reason through a moral dilemma out loud, identifying the competing goods and the costs of each option
The student can identify moments in their own life where they practiced courage, honesty, or fairness --- and moments where they fell short
The student engages with moral stories as genuine investigations, not as lessons with predetermined answers
The student shows growing comfort with moral complexity --- the idea that not every question has an easy answer

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time when doing the right thing was hard.
narration_prompts.detail: What made it hard? What did you do? What would you do differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a story where a character had to choose between two good things.
narration_prompts.detail: What were the good things in conflict? What did the character choose? Was it the right choice?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between treating people the same and treating people fairly?
narration_prompts.detail: Give an example where they are different.
Not just the dictionary definition --- what does it actually look like in real life? Give an example from a story and an example from your own experience.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a character from a story who made a choice you disagree with.
narration_prompts.detail: What would you have done instead? Why?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean that "character is what you do when no one is watching"?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is that different from doing the right thing when people are watching?

cross_connections.description: The character formation work in IC1.2 runs directly alongside this packet. The virtues discussed here --- honesty, courage, fairness --- are the same virtues being developed in the self-governance track.
The fairness instinct discussed here is the same instinct that drives the creation of governance and law.
The fairness concepts here connect directly to the study of rules, consequences, and restitution in 1.4.3.
The moral frameworks of different cultures and religions --- studied in 1.3.1 --- provide different answers to the ethical questions raised here.
Narrating moral dilemmas and defending choices builds the same analytical writing skills developed in 3.2.1.
The self-discipline to do the right thing when it is hard is built on the same foundation as the self-discipline to complete tasks and follow through on commitments.

content.why_this_matters:
You already know the difference between right and wrong. You know it is wrong to hit someone. You know it is wrong to take something that is not yours. You know it is wrong to lie. These things feel obvious --- and they are, in the simple cases.

But what happens when the choices are not simple? What happens when you have to choose between two good things and you cannot have both? What happens when telling the truth will hurt someone's feelings? What happens when the right thing to do is also the hard thing to do? What happens when you are not sure what the right thing is?

This packet is about learning to think through the hard choices --- not just the easy ones. You are going to discover that doing right sometimes costs something, that fairness is more complicated than "everyone gets the same," and that good people can disagree about what is right. You are going to build the beginning of a skill that will serve you for the rest of your life: the ability to think carefully about what you should do when the answer is not obvious.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The concept that doing right sometimes costs something** --- virtue is not free; choosing well when it is costly is different from choosing well when it is easy
2. **The ability to see multiple sides of a disagreement** --- good people can disagree; understanding why someone made a choice before judging it
3. **Fairness as a complex idea** --- equal treatment is not always fair treatment; the student begins to see this through examples
4. **The habit of asking "why?" before judging** --- understanding the reasons behind a choice before evaluating it
5. **Stories as moral laboratories** --- using narrative to encounter moral complexity in a safe context
6. **The beginning of ethical vocabulary** --- right, wrong, fair, unfair, just, unjust, honest, courageous
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Easy Right and Hard Right

Some choices are easy. If you find a wallet with money in it and you know whose it is, the right thing to do is return it. You know this. It is obvious.

But what if you really need the money? What if your family is struggling and the wallet contains a hundred dollars and the person who lost it is very wealthy? The right thing to do is still to return it --- but now it costs you something. The choice is harder. And the fact that you make the right choice when it is hard tells you more about your character than making the right choice when it is easy.

This is one of the most important ideas in this packet: **doing right when it costs nothing is not the same as doing right when it costs something.** The person who tells the truth when there is no penalty for lying is being truthful. The person who tells the truth when telling the truth will get them in trouble is being courageous. Both are good, but they are not the same.

### Part 2: When Good People Disagree

Here is something that may surprise you: sometimes two people can both be trying to do the right thing and disagree about what the right thing is.

Imagine your family finds a stray dog. Your mother says: "We should keep it. It needs a home, and we can take care of it." Your father says: "We should take it to the shelter. Someone may be looking for it, and we already have too many responsibilities." Who is right?

Both of them are thinking about what is good. Your mother is thinking about compassion --- the dog needs care. Your father is thinking about responsibility --- they have existing commitments and the original owner may be searching. They are not arguing because one of them is selfish or mean. They are arguing because different good things --- compassion and responsibility --- are pulling in different directions.

This is what adults call a "moral dilemma" --- a situation where there is no perfect answer because genuine goods are in conflict. You will encounter these throughout your life. The skill you need is not knowing the right answer in advance. It is the ability to see what is good on each side before you decide.

### Part 3: The Cost of Courage

Courage is not the absence of fear. Courage is doing the right thing even when you are afraid.

The child who stands up for a classmate being bullied --- when standing up might make the bully target them instead --- is showing courage. The child who tells the truth about something they did wrong --- when lying would be easier --- is showing courage. The child who refuses to go along with the group when the group is doing something wrong --- even though it means being left out --- is showing courage.

Every culture in the world has stories about courage. The hero who faces the dragon. The small person who stands up to the powerful one. The honest person who tells the truth when everyone else is lying. These stories exist because every culture knows that courage is one of the most important qualities a person can develop --- and one of the hardest.

You do not become courageous by reading about courage. You become courageous by practicing courage --- by making the hard choices in small things, day after day, until it becomes part of who you are.

### Part 4: Fairness When Things Are Not Equal

You have already begun to learn that equal treatment is not always fair treatment. Now let's think about this more carefully.

Imagine you have two plants. One is in a sunny window and gets plenty of light. The other is in a dark corner and gets almost none. If you give both plants the same amount of water, are you treating them fairly? You are treating them equally --- but the plant in the dark corner may need more water because it is already struggling, or it may need to be moved to the light.

Fair treatment sometimes means giving different people different things based on their different needs. A child who cannot see well needs glasses --- it would not be "fair" to say "nobody gets glasses because not everyone needs them." A child who has trouble reading needs extra help --- it would not be "fair" to say "everyone gets the same amount of reading instruction" if that means the struggling child falls further behind.

This is a hard idea because it seems to contradict the rule "treat everyone the same." But the deeper principle is not "treat everyone the same" --- it is "treat everyone justly." And justice sometimes requires treating people differently.

### Part 5: What Would You Do? Thinking Through Hard Choices

Here are some situations for you to think about. There are no easy answers --- that is the point. The goal is not to find "the right answer" but to practice thinking carefully about hard choices.

**Scenario 1:** Your best friend did something wrong, and a teacher asks you if you know who did it. If you tell the truth, your friend will be in serious trouble. If you stay silent, someone else might be blamed. What do you do?

**Scenario 2:** You promised to play with one friend, but then another friend --- who is having a really bad day and seems very sad --- asks you to play with them instead. You can only play with one. What do you do?

**Scenario 3:** You see an older kid being mean to a younger kid. You could say something, but the older kid might turn on you. You could tell an adult, but the older kid might call you a tattletale. You could do nothing, but the younger kid is being hurt. What do you do?

In each case, think about: What are the different good things pulling in different directions? What would happen with each choice? What would it cost? Is there a way to honor more than one good thing at the same time?

### Part 6: Character Is What You Do When No One Is Watching

There is a saying that character is what you do when no one is watching. This is one of the truest things you will ever hear.

When other people are watching, you have an incentive to behave well --- you want to be thought of well, you want to avoid punishment, you want to look good. These are real motivations, but they are external --- they come from outside you.

Character is different. Character is the quality that makes you do the right thing even when there is no audience, no reward, and no punishment for doing wrong. The person with character returns the wallet even when no one would know if they kept the money. They tell the truth even when lying would be easier and undetectable. They keep their promise even when breaking it would go unnoticed.

Building character is one of the central purposes of this entire curriculum. Every packet in IC1 (your self-governance work) is building it. Every time you do the right thing when it is hard, you are building it. Every time you fall short and try again, you are building it. Character is not something you have or do not have --- it is something you build, one choice at a time.

### Part 7: Stories That Teach Without Telling

The best moral stories do not tell you what to think. They show you a situation and let you figure it out.

*The Giving Tree* does not tell you whether the tree is generous or foolish. It shows you a relationship and lets you decide. *Each Kindness* does not lecture you about being kind. It shows you what it feels like to miss the chance. The folktales of every culture do not list rules --- they present characters making choices and living with the results.

This is how moral reasoning develops. Not through lectures about right and wrong, but through encounters with situations that require thought. Every time you read a story and ask "was that the right choice?" you are practicing the skill of ethical reasoning. Every time you disagree with someone about what a character should have done, you are discovering that moral questions are real questions with genuine complexity.

### Part 8: The Beginning of Wisdom

The beginning of wisdom is knowing that you do not know everything --- and that hard choices require careful thought.

The fool thinks every question is easy. The fool thinks they already know what is right in every situation. The wise person knows that some questions are genuinely hard, that good people can disagree, and that the right answer sometimes depends on details that only become clear when you think carefully.

You are at the very beginning of a journey that will take your entire life. The ethical questions you encounter in this packet --- about honesty, courage, fairness, and hard choices --- are the same questions the greatest philosophers in history have wrestled with. You are joining a conversation that is thousands of years old. Your job right now is not to have all the answers. Your job is to develop the habit of thinking carefully, honestly, and courageously about what is right --- and then doing it.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Hard Choice Stories [Ongoing]
Read stories involving genuine moral difficulty from at least three cultures. After each story, discuss: what was the character's choice? What good things were in conflict? Would you have chosen differently? Why? The goal is the habit of moral analysis through narrative.

### Study 2: The Cost of Honesty [Early Packet]
Read *The Empty Pot*. Discuss: why was Ping's honesty remarkable? It would have been easy to lie. What made his choice hard? What would have happened if he had lied? Then discuss a time in the student's own life when being honest cost them something.

### Study 3: The Missed Kindness [Mid-Packet]
Read *Each Kindness*. Discuss: what happened because Chloe missed the chance to be kind? Could she fix it later? What does this tell you about the importance of acting when you have the chance? The concept of moral regret.

### Study 4: The Hard Scenarios [Mid-Packet]
Work through the scenarios from Part 5 together. The student reasons through each one out loud. The mentor does not give answers but asks questions: "What would happen if...?" "Is there another way?" "What does it cost?"

### Study 5: Fairness vs. Equality Exploration [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Present scenarios where equal treatment is unfair and where unequal treatment is fair. The student identifies the principle: justice sometimes requires treating people differently based on their different needs. Discuss: how do you decide when different treatment is fair and when it is not?

### Study 6: Character Building Reflection [Late Packet]
The student identifies three moments in their recent life when they had to make a choice between easy-and-wrong and hard-and-right. For each, they narrate: what was the choice? What did they do? Were they satisfied with their choice? What would they do differently? This is not a confession --- it is a self-assessment.

### Study 7: The Giving Tree Debate [End of Packet]
Read *The Giving Tree*. Then hold a genuine debate: is the tree generous or exploited? Is the boy selfish or simply growing up? Is the relationship healthy? There should be no "right answer" --- the point is the quality of the reasoning.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Moral Complexity Awareness
**Developing well:** The student recognizes that some moral questions are genuinely hard. They can identify competing goods in a dilemma. They do not rush to judgment.
**Developing slowly:** The student sees every moral question as simple. "Just be honest." "Just be nice." No awareness of competing goods or costs.
**Diagnostic:** Have you presented enough genuinely hard cases? If all the moral scenarios are simple, the student has no reason to develop complexity awareness.

### 2. Perspective-Taking
**Developing well:** Before judging a character's choice, the student asks why they made it. They can articulate the other side's perspective even when they disagree.
**Developing slowly:** Judges immediately without considering the character's reasons.
**Diagnostic:** Model the question: "Before we decide if that was right or wrong, let's understand why they did it." Repeated modeling builds the habit.

### 3. Cost of Virtue Recognition
**Developing well:** Understands that doing right can be costly. Can articulate what a character (or they themselves) gave up to do the right thing.
**Developing slowly:** Sees virtue as cost-free. Does not recognize the sacrifice involved in hard choices.

### 4. Fairness Sophistication
**Developing well:** Can distinguish between equal and fair. Can give examples where different treatment is more just.
**Developing slowly:** Equates fairness with equal treatment in all cases.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Moralizing
If you find yourself delivering sermons about right and wrong, stop. The packet is about developing the student's reasoning capacity, not about transmitting moral conclusions. Let the stories do the work. Let the discussions be genuine explorations, not catechism.

### Avoiding Genuine Dilemmas
It is tempting to present only cases with clear right answers. Resist this. The student needs cases where reasonable people disagree, where both options have costs, and where the "right" choice is not obvious. These cases --- not the easy ones --- develop moral reasoning.

### Resolving Debates Too Quickly
When the student and the mentor disagree about a story (Was the Giving Tree generous or foolish?), the temptation is to provide the "correct" interpretation. Do not. Let the disagreement stand. Let the student sit with the fact that thoughtful people can reach different conclusions. This is itself a moral lesson: moral complexity is real.

### Neglecting the Character Building Component
Part 6 (character is what you do when no one is watching) and Study 6 (the character building reflection) are essential. Moral reasoning is not just an intellectual exercise --- it is preparation for action. The student who can reason about hard choices but never applies that reasoning to their own behavior has developed only half the skill.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Doing the Hard Right Thing
**Strong:** Describes a specific situation with detail. Articulates what made it hard. Reflects honestly on what they did and what they might do differently.
**Weak:** "I'm always honest." No specificity, no genuine reflection.

### Prompt 2: Character's Hard Choice
**Strong:** Identifies the specific competing goods. Explains the character's reasoning before evaluating it. May offer an alternative approach.
**Weak:** "The character was wrong." No analysis of competing goods.

### Prompt 3: Equal vs. Fair
**Strong:** Gives a clear, specific example. Articulates the principle: justice sometimes requires different treatment.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between the two concepts.

### Prompt 4: Courage
**Strong:** Defines courage in terms of action despite fear or cost. Gives specific examples from both stories and personal experience.
**Weak:** Defines courage as "being brave" without specificity.
